start.title: A Danish Boy In Theresienstadt
start.subtitle: A Drawn Testimony
start.text:
On October 2, 1943, eight-year-old Steen Metz and his parents were arrested in their home in Odense, Denmark, and deported to Theresienstadt Concentration Camp.
What followed were eighteen months of hunger, brutality, and loss—including the death of his father from starvation. Of the 15,000 children who passed through Theresienstadt, fewer than 1,500 survived. Steen was one of them. For decades, like many Holocaust survivors, Steen kept his experiences private. But in 2011, he was ready to share his story through his memoir, A Danish Boy in Theresienstadt.
Now, we retell Steen's journey through an interactive visual experience, where hand-drawn illustrations guide you through his memories. This isn't just a story to read—it's a story to witness, to explore, and to remember.

young.title: Steen Metz
young.subtitle: An Almost Normal Life
young.text:
Steen and his parents lived in the Stovesberg apartment complex at Hjallesevej 98 in Odense, Denmark. Despite food rationing under the occupation, the family maintained an almost normal daily life.

Click on the interactive objects in the drawing to find out more.

steenphoto_x5F_group.title: Steen Metz
steenphoto_x5F_group.subtitle: The Summer Before the Arrest
steenphoto_x5F_group.text:
Pictured here at the age of eight, Steen is photographed eating ice cream during the summer before his family's arrest. He later recalled the relative normalcy of those years:

quote(I cycled to and from school. I often met Nazi soldiers – they never spoke to me or touched me.)

familyphoto_x5F_group.title: Steen Metz
familyphoto_x5F_group.subtitle: Living at Stovesberg
familyphoto_x5F_group.text:
This photograph shows Steen Axel Metz with his father, Axel Mogens Metz, and his grandmother, seated on a stone wall outside their apartment on Hjallesevej. Steen retained fond memories of his father, who taught him to ride a bicycle and tended a garden allotment around the corner from their home, separate from the Stovesberg complex itself. Steen later reflected on the time they spent there together:

quote(My father had a garden 'commune', since we always lived in apartments without a garden. I have fond memories of the time I spent with my father planting vegetables and flowers from seeds. I even sold some of the final products to my mother. The beginning of my business career!)

hjallesevej.title: The Arrest
hjallesevej.text:
In April 1940, the same month German forces occupied Denmark, nine-year-old Steen and his family moved into a new apartment on Hjallesevej in Odense. Life continued as normal, Steen helped the milkman with his deliveries, and in 1942 he started school. But by the time he reached third class, Steen and his parents were arrested by the Nazis and taken from their home.

Click on the interactive objects in the drawing to find out more.

transport.title: Transport to Theresienstadt
transport.subtitle: A Three-Day Journey
transport.text:
Following their arrest, Steen and his parents were loaded aboard a cattle car and transported to Theresienstadt. The journey lasted three days, during which the car remained locked from the outside and stopped only once, when a small amount of water was provided. Among the passengers were four children, including Steen, who were able to lie down. The adults had to alternate between sitting and standing.

Click on the interactive objects in the drawing to find out more.

map.title: From Odense to Theresienstadt with the borders of 1938.

cattlecar_x5F_group.title: The Cattle Car
cattlecar_x5F_group.subtitle: Transport to Theresienstadt
cattlecar_x5F_group.text: The conditions aboard the transport were poor. Although historians commonly employ the term 'boxcar', Steen and fellow survivors consistently use the term 'cattle car', because that is what it was. The absence of any sanitary facilities produced an overwhelming smell, which Steen remembers vividly. Steen recounts the frightened faces around him, no one had any knowledge of what would happen to them.

roof_x5F_group.title: Station Bauschowitz-Theresienstadt
roof_x5F_group.subtitle: Transport to Theresienstadt
roof_x5F_group.text:
Theresienstadt had no railway station of its own. Instead, deportees were brought to the station Bauschowitz-Theresienstadt, known today as Bohušovice train station. From here, they were required to walk one and a half miles to reach the camp. Upon arrival, prisoners were ordered to surrender all valuables, including jewellery and money that they had been encouraged to bring. Steen's mother was permitted to retain her wedding ring solely because it could not be removed from her finger.

Although the newly arrived prisoners were separated into groups based on gender and age, Steen was permitted to remain with his mother during his time at Theresienstadt.

barracks.title: Forced Labour
barracks.subtitle: Living Conditions in Theresienstadt
barracks.text:
A drawing depicted here illustrates the living conditions that Steen and his family endured during their internment at Theresienstadt. They were housed in a loft, where the poor environment posed challenges throughout the year: summers brought infestations of lice and fleas, while winters were marked by extreme cold.

Click on the interactive objects in the drawing to find out more.

mother_x5F_group.title: Forced Labour
mother_x5F_group.subtitle: Mica Production
mother_x5F_group.text: According to Steen’s testimony, his mother was not subjected to physical abuse in Theresienstadt. In addition to childcare duties, she was assigned to factory work, producing insulation material for airplanes from mica. On one occasion, after telling a soldier that she was faring poorly following her husband’s death from malnutrition, the soldier returned each day for eight days, compelling her to state that her husband had died of pneumonia. On a separate occasion, a guard deliberately pushed over a bucket of dirty water while she was cleaning floors, forcing her to start over.

father_x5F_group.title: Forced Labour
father_x5F_group.subtitle: Road Construction
father_x5F_group.text:
Prior to his arrest and deportation to Theresienstadt in October 1943, Steen's father, Axel Mogens Metz, had practised as a lawyer. Within the camp, Axel was assigned to road-construction labour, physically demanding work for which he had no prior preparation. Working days were long, with minimal rest periods and little food. On one occasion, a guard confiscated his coat stating that it was unsuitable for manual labour.

Steen only learned after the war that his father had been subjected to physical abuse during his time at Theresienstadt.

death_x5F_group.title: Forced Labour
death_x5F_group.subtitle: The Death of Steen’s Father
death_x5F_group.text:
The toll of the camp conditions caused Axel Mogens Metz to lose about fifty percent of his body weight. Although the camp infirmary’s medical staff performed their duties thoroughly, they lacked the supplies necessary to provide adequate care. On 13 March 1944, Steen’s father died of malnutrition at the age of forty; the Nazi authorities recorded the cause of death as pneumonia.

In a later interview, Steen’s mother recounted that she was required to visit seventeen separate locations before receiving confirmation of her husband’s death.

soccer.title: Playing Soccer
soccer.subtitle: Life in Theresienstadt
soccer.text:
Steen was only nine years old when he was held at Theresienstadt. Amid queuing for food and delivering messages in the camp, he found moments of friendship and childhood while playing soccer. In his testimony, Steen recalled that these were the only moments in which he felt closest to being a nine-year-old boy.

Click on the interactive objects in the drawing to find out more.

ball_x5F_group.title: The Soccer Field
ball_x5F_group.subtitle: Playing Soccer
ball_x5F_group.text: Steen played with a group of Czech children. In order to communicate with them, he learned Czech phonetically. The conditions in which Steen and his Czech friends played were makeshift. There was no soccer field, no designated goal post, and no proper ball; the games took place on a gravel field, and Steen's mother made a ball from old rags – one that did not last long.

steenmetz_x5F_group.title: Friendships in Theresienstadt
steenmetz_x5F_group.subtitle: Playing Soccer
steenmetz_x5F_group.text: Besides lining up for food and doing voluntary work, Steen played soccer with some of the Czech children in Theresienstadt. To communicate with them, he learned Czech phonetically. Steen recalls that these were the only moments where he felt closest to being a nine-year-old.

friends_x5F_group.title: Friendships in Theresienstadt
friends_x5F_group.subtitle: Playing Soccer
friends_x5F_group.text: On one occasion, Steen arrived at the field to play soccer to find that his Czech friends had not come. His mother, who knew what had happened, comforted him and chose to protect him from the truth: the children had been transported to extermination camps.

whitebus.title: Happier Times
whitebus.subtitle: Liberation by the White Busses
whitebus.text:
In April 1945, a few weeks before the end of the war, prisoners in Theresienstadt heard rumours that the White Busses were on their way from Sweden to liberate the Danish Jews. Although Sweden was a neutral country, the White Busses arrived safely two days later, on 15 April. It was a miracle that they had been able to travel through Germany unharmed.

Click on the interactive objects in the drawing to find out more.

map.title: From Theresienstadt to Odense with the borders of 1938.

whitebus_x5F_group.title: Happier Times
whitebus_x5F_group.subtitle: Liberation by the White Busses
whitebus_x5F_group.text: The White Busses rescued approximately 425 surviving Danish Jews. Before departure, eight young Danish men married women from other countries so that they could also be liberated. Among those who left were four babies who had been born in the camp in secret. On the way back, the White Busses had to make several stops. Steen still remembers the sound of bombardments and the faces of young soldiers along the road.

crowd_x5F_group.title: Happier Times
crowd_x5F_group.subtitle: Liberation by the White Busses
crowd_x5F_group.text: When the White Busses reached the Danish border on 17 April, Denmark was still under occupation. Nonetheless, the rescued Jews were greeted with flowers, candy, snacks, and sandwiches. The White Busses continued to Sweden, where the liberated prisoners spent around eight days in quarantine.

modelcamp.title: The Beautification Program
modelcamp.subtitle: The Model Camp
modelcamp.text:
In 1944, the International Committee of the Red Cross, accompanied by representatives of the Danish administration, requested access to the camp to assess the treatment of Danish Jewish prisoners. As far as Steen knows, there were no requests from other countries to see the camp. The Nazi authorities recognised the visit as a propaganda opportunity, giving Theresienstadt the nickname 'model camp'.

Click on the interactive objects in the drawing to find out more.

pavillion_x5F_group.title: A Visit from the Danish Delegation
pavillion_x5F_group.subtitle: The Beautification Program
pavillion_x5F_group.text:
In preparation for the inspection, the Nazis initiated the beautification programme in the beginning of February 1944. Streets and buildings were cleaned, flower beds were laid out, and recreational spaces were constructed. To reduce the appearance of overcrowding, approximately 7,500 prisoners were deported to Auschwitz prior to the visit.

The inspection took place in the summer of 1944, lasting between four and eight hours. The visit had been carefully rehearsed in advance. Prisoners were directed to perform assigned activities, hard labour was suspended, and Danish Jews were relocated to better quarters. The prisoners were explicitly forbidden from speaking to the delegation or asking questions.
